metaTitle: Court Divorce Under Muslim Law Bangladesh 2026 Guide
title: Divorce from Court Under Muslim Law in Bangladesh: Judicial Khul, Dissolution, and Decree
metaDescription: Complete guide to court divorce under Muslim law in Bangladesh — judicial khul, dissolution of marriage, court procedure, maintenance, and child custody after divorce.
heroIntro: While Muslim law in Bangladesh provides several mechanisms for out-of-court divorce — including talaq by the husband and khul by mutual agreement — there are many situations where obtaining a court-ordered divorce (judicial dissolution of marriage) is the only viable option. Whether you are a wife seeking a judicial dissolution on grounds of cruelty, desertion, or failure to maintain, or a husband seeking the court's assistance to formalise or enforce a divorce, this guide covers the complete legal process for court divorce under Muslim law in Bangladesh.

quickAnswer.heading: ⚡ Quick Answer: Court Divorce Under Muslim Law Bangladesh
points: A wife can seek judicial dissolution of marriage under the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939.
Grounds include: failure of husband to maintain, cruelty, desertion, imprisonment, insanity, and others.
Khul divorce (wife-initiated) can also be obtained through court if the husband agrees to return the mahr.
File the case at the Family Court (Senior Assistant Judge's Court) in the district.
The court can also determine maintenance, mahr, and child custody in the same proceedings.

sections.h2: Overview of Court Divorce Under Muslim Law Bangladesh
sections.content:
In Bangladesh, divorce among Muslim couples is governed by Islamic personal law as codified and applied by Bangladeshi courts, primarily through the following statutes:

  • Muslim Family Laws Ordinance, 1961 (MFLO): Regulates talaq (husband-initiated divorce), khul (wife-initiated divorce), and registration requirements.
  • Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939 (DMMA): Provides Muslim wives with the statutory right to seek dissolution of marriage from the court on specified grounds.
  • Family Courts Ordinance, 1985: Provides the procedural framework for family disputes, including divorce, maintenance, dower, and custody, before the Family Court.
  • Muslim Marriages and Divorces (Registration) Act, 1974: Requires registration of marriages and divorces with the relevant government authority (the Kazi).

Court involvement in a Muslim divorce typically occurs in the following situations:

  • A wife seeks judicial dissolution of the marriage under the DMMA;
  • A dispute arises over the validity or completion of a talaq;
  • The wife seeks khul divorce with return of mahr and requires court enforcement;
  • Disputes over maintenance, mahr, or child custody accompany the divorce;
  • One party challenges the divorce as invalid or incomplete.

Understanding which legal route applies to your situation is the first step. Consult a qualified divorce lawyer in Bangladesh immediately for personalised advice.

sections.h2: The Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939: Grounds for Divorce
sections.content:
The Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939 (DMMA) is the primary legislation enabling Muslim wives in Bangladesh to seek court-ordered dissolution of their marriage. This Act was groundbreaking at the time of its enactment as it gave Muslim women statutory grounds for divorce — rights that were previously very limited under classical Islamic law as applied in colonial courts.

Under Section 2 of the DMMA, a Muslim wife can seek dissolution on any of the following grounds:

  1. Whereabouts unknown for 4 years: The husband's whereabouts have been unknown for a period of four years. The wife must first advertise in newspapers and give notice to the last known relatives of the husband before the court grants the decree.
  2. Failure to provide maintenance for 2 years: The husband has failed to provide maintenance for the wife for a period of two years.
  3. Imprisonment for 7 years or more: The husband has been sentenced to imprisonment for a period of seven years or more.
  4. Failure to perform marital obligations for 3 years: The husband has failed to perform his marital obligations without reasonable cause for three years.
  5. Impotency: The husband was impotent at the time of the marriage and continues to be so. The court may allow the husband 1 year to prove his potency before granting the decree.
  6. Insanity or serious disease: The husband has been insane for 2 years, or is suffering from leprosy or a virulent venereal disease.
  7. Marriage before puberty (option of puberty): The wife was given in marriage by her father or guardian before attaining puberty and she repudiates the marriage before age 18, provided the marriage has not been consummated.
  8. Cruelty: The husband treats her with cruelty, which includes: habitual assault or cruelty that makes life miserable; association with women of ill repute; compelling her to lead an immoral life; disposing of her property or preventing her from exercising legal rights; obstructing her in the observance of religious practice; failure to treat her equitably with co-wives (where applicable); any other form of cruelty as defined by the court.
  9. Other grounds recognised by Muslim law: Any other ground that is recognised as valid for divorce under Muslim law.

Khul is the Islamic form of divorce initiated by the wife, in which she offers to return the mahr (dower) or some other consideration to the husband in exchange for the dissolution of the marriage. Unlike a DMMA dissolution (which requires proof of grounds), khul does not require any specific ground — the wife only needs to establish that she cannot live with the husband.

Under classical Islamic law and Bangladesh court practice, khul requires the husband's agreement. However, where the husband is unwilling to agree, the court can grant a judicial khul in the following circumstances:

  • The wife establishes that the marriage has irretrievably broken down and she cannot live with the husband in the bounds prescribed by Allah;
  • She is willing to return the mahr or other agreed consideration;
  • The court, after counselling and conciliation attempts, is satisfied that reconciliation is impossible.

In Bangladesh, Family Courts regularly grant khul divorces even where the husband opposes them, applying the principle that a woman cannot be compelled to remain in a marriage against her will if she is willing to compensate the husband for the dissolution.

Key points about khul:

  • The wife typically returns the mahr received at the time of marriage;
  • If no mahr was specified or paid, the court determines a fair compensation;
  • The husband retains the right to three reconciliation periods (though courts do not always enforce this strictly in judicial khul cases);
  • Khul must be registered with the Kazi after the decree is passed.

sections.h2: Talaq by the Husband: Procedure and Registration
sections.content:
While this guide focuses on court divorce, it is important to understand the husband's talaq procedure under Bangladesh law, as legal disputes often arise from improperly executed talaq:

  • Giving Talaq: Under the MFLO 1961, a husband who wishes to pronounce talaq must, as soon as practicable after pronouncing talaq in any form, give written notice to the Chairman of the Union Council (or ward commissioner in city areas) where the marriage was registered, and send a copy to the wife.
  • Reconciliation Period: The Union Council Chairman constitutes an Arbitration Council of two representatives (one from each side) within 30 days of receiving notice. The Council attempts reconciliation for up to 90 days.
  • Talaq Becomes Effective: If reconciliation fails and the divorce is not withdrawn, the talaq becomes effective after the 90-day period.
  • Without Notice: A talaq pronounced without giving the required MFLO notice is invalid in Bangladesh law — a crucial protection for wives whose husbands pronounce informal talaq (including 'triple talaq' in one sitting) without following procedure.
  • Registration: After the talaq becomes effective, it must be registered under the Muslim Marriages and Divorces (Registration) Act, 1974.

Court disputes over talaq arise when: the husband disputes that the talaq was withdrawn; the wife disputes that proper notice was given; or there is a dispute about the effective date of the talaq for purposes of iddat, maintenance, and custody.

sections.h2: How to File a Divorce Case in Family Court Bangladesh
sections.content:
Whether filing under the DMMA for judicial dissolution or for a khul divorce, the court procedure is similar. Here is the step-by-step process:

  1. Consult a Family Lawyer: Engage an experienced divorce lawyer in Bangladesh who will assess your grounds for divorce and advise on the optimal legal route.
  2. Draft the Plaint (Narishta): The family lawyer drafts a formal plaint (suit) setting out the grounds for dissolution, the history of the marriage, details of the nikah (marriage contract), the mahr amount, children (if any), and the relief sought.
  3. File at the Family Court: File the suit at the Family Court (Senior Assistant Judge's Court) in the district where the wife resides or where the parties last cohabited. Pay the required court fee.
  4. Court Serves Notice on Husband: The court issues summons to the husband (defendant) requiring him to file a written statement (defence) within the specified time.
  5. Mediation/Reconciliation Attempt: Under the Family Courts Ordinance, courts must attempt reconciliation before proceeding to trial. The court may adjourn the matter for the parties to attempt mediation. If reconciliation fails, the case proceeds to trial.
  6. Evidence Stage: Both parties file affidavit evidence and present oral witnesses. Documents (nikah nama, maintenance payment records, medical evidence of cruelty, etc.) are exhibited.
  7. Arguments: Lawyers for both sides make oral and written arguments.
  8. Decree: The court passes a decree of dissolution of marriage. The decree is forwarded to the relevant Kazi for registration.
  9. Iddat Period: After the decree, the wife observes the iddat (waiting period) of approximately three months before she can remarry.

sections.h2: Maintenance Rights of the Wife After Divorce
sections.content:
Maintenance (nafaqa) is a critical financial right of a divorced wife under Muslim law in Bangladesh. Understanding the scope and duration of maintenance rights is essential for both parties:

  • Maintenance During Marriage: The husband must maintain the wife throughout the marriage. Failure to maintain is a ground for judicial dissolution under the DMMA.
  • Maintenance During Iddat: After divorce — whether by talaq or court dissolution — the wife is entitled to maintenance from the husband throughout the iddat period (approximately 3 months or until delivery of child if pregnant).
  • Mata'a (Post-Divorce Consolation Gift): Under Islamic law, the husband is also obliged to give a consolation gift (mata'a) to the divorced wife, the amount of which is assessed by the court taking into account the husband's means and the circumstances of the divorce.
  • Mahr/Dower: Unpaid mahr (dower promised at marriage) becomes immediately payable in full upon divorce. The wife can separately sue for recovery of her mahr if it has not been paid.
  • Children's Maintenance: The husband remains obliged to pay maintenance for the children regardless of which parent has custody. The court fixes the amount based on the husband's income and the children's needs.

Maintenance disputes can be resolved within the same divorce proceedings in the Family Court, saving the parties the cost and time of separate litigation. Your divorce lawyer can include maintenance, mahr, and custody prayers in the same plaint.

sections.h2: Child Custody Arrangements in Divorce Proceedings
sections.content:
Divorce proceedings frequently include disputes over the custody and guardianship of children. The Family Court has jurisdiction to make custody and visitation orders within the same divorce proceedings, avoiding the need for separate guardianship proceedings in most cases.

The key principles applied by Bangladesh courts in divorce-related custody disputes are:

  • Welfare of the Child: The paramount consideration in all custody decisions is the welfare of the child — physical, emotional, educational, and moral.
  • Hizanat Rules: Under Muslim law, the mother has the right of hizanat for sons up to 7 years and daughters until puberty (see our detailed Muslim law custody guide). These rules apply unless the court determines that the welfare of the child requires otherwise.
  • Visitation for Non-Custodial Parent: Courts regularly make detailed visitation orders specifying the non-custodial parent's access to the child — including weekly visits, holiday stays, and telephone/video contact.
  • Interim Custody Orders: During the divorce proceedings, the court can make interim custody orders to regulate the child's care while the case is ongoing.

If custody is genuinely contested, the court may appoint a guardian ad litem or interview the child in chambers to determine their wishes (for children of sufficient age and maturity). Detailed guidance on child custody can be found in our child custody guide for 2026.

Mahr (also called dower) is the mandatory gift of money or property that the husband must give to the wife at the time of marriage or at a later agreed date. It is a fundamental right of the wife under Muslim law and its recovery is enforceable through court proceedings.

Types of mahr under Bangladesh Muslim law:

  • Prompt Mahr (Muajjal): Payable immediately upon demand by the wife — either at the time of marriage or whenever the wife demands it during the marriage.
  • Deferred Mahr (Muajjal/Mu'ajjal): Payable at a specified future date, upon death of the husband, or upon divorce — whichever occurs first.

Recovery of mahr through court:

  • If the husband fails to pay prompt mahr on demand, the wife can file a suit in the Family Court for recovery of the mahr amount as a debt due.
  • Upon divorce, all unpaid mahr becomes immediately payable. If not paid, the wife can sue for recovery in the Family Court.
  • The mahr suit can be filed as part of the divorce proceedings or as a separate suit if divorce has already been decreed.
  • Courts have authority to issue injunctions freezing the husband's assets pending payment of mahr in appropriate cases.

The nikah nama (marriage contract) is the primary evidence of the agreed mahr amount. Always keep a certified copy of your nikah nama safely. If you need assistance recovering your mahr, a family lawyer in Bangladesh can file the appropriate recovery suit on your behalf.

sections.h2: Timeline and Practical Expectations of Divorce Proceedings
sections.content:
Understanding the realistic timeline of a court divorce in Bangladesh helps both parties plan and make informed decisions:

  • Uncontested Divorce: If both parties agree to the divorce, the grounds are clear, and there are no major disputes over maintenance, mahr, or custody, a Family Court divorce decree can be obtained in 3 to 9 months.
  • Contested Divorce: If the husband contests the divorce or disputes the grounds, the case will require full trial — examination of witnesses, cross-examination, and legal arguments. This can take 1 to 4 years at the Family Court level.
  • Appeals: Either party can appeal the Family Court's decision to the District Judge's Court and then to the High Court Division. Appeals can add further years to the final resolution.
  • Interim Maintenance: The wife can apply for interim maintenance (iddat maintenance and child maintenance) at an early stage of the proceedings — courts typically fix interim maintenance within the first few hearings.
  • Practical Tips:
  1. Collect and preserve all evidence of marriage (nikah nama), mahr, payments, and the breakdown of the marriage before filing.
  2. If there are minor children, gather evidence of your ability to provide a stable home environment — school records, home ownership, income documents.
  3. Consider attempting mediation before resorting to full court proceedings — a negotiated settlement is faster, less expensive, and less traumatic for the children.
  4. Ensure your divorce is registered with the Kazi after the decree is passed — an unregistered divorce can cause legal complications for future marriage and for the children's status.

faqs.question: Can a Muslim woman in Bangladesh get a divorce without the husband's consent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Under the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939, a Muslim wife can obtain a court-ordered dissolution of marriage based on specific statutory grounds (cruelty, desertion, failure to maintain, etc.) without the husband's consent. The court grants the decree after satisfying itself that the grounds are established, regardless of the husband's opposition.

faqs.question: What is the difference between talaq and judicial dissolution in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Talaq is a unilateral act of divorce by the husband under Muslim law, which becomes effective after following the MFLO 1961 notice procedure. Judicial dissolution is a court-ordered divorce obtained by the wife (or either party in a khul case) through the Family Court under the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939 or the Family Courts Ordinance 1985. Judicial dissolution requires court proceedings and a decree.

faqs.question: How much mahr should I include in the nikah nama?
faqs.answer: There is no legally prescribed minimum mahr amount in Bangladesh, though Islamic scholars and courts recommend that it should be a meaningful sum that provides genuine financial security to the wife. The mahr amount should be agreed between the parties before the nikah and clearly recorded in the nikah nama. Once agreed and recorded, it is a legally enforceable debt.

faqs.question: Can I get maintenance from my husband even after divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Under Muslim law, a divorced wife is entitled to maintenance during the iddat period (approximately 3 months) and to unpaid mahr. The husband's maintenance obligation ends after iddat, unless a court order extends it in special circumstances. However, the husband's obligation to maintain the children continues beyond iddat until they reach adulthood.

faqs.question: What happens if my husband refuses to appear in court for the divorce case?
faqs.answer: If the husband (defendant) is properly served with summons but fails to appear, the Family Court can proceed ex parte — meaning the court hears and decides the case based on the wife's evidence alone, without the husband's participation. An ex parte decree for dissolution of marriage can be granted if the evidence establishes the grounds. The husband retains the right to apply to set aside the ex parte decree if he has a valid reason for non-appearance.
